Thunderstorm winds downed a few trees near Fellsburg.
Thunderstorm winds downed power lines in Stahlstown.
Power lines were downed by thunderstorm winds in Everson.

A severe thunderstorm took down numerous power lines and some trees and consequently caused power outages to about 80,000 Philadelphia Electric Company (PECO) homes in central and southern Bucks County.  Trees were uprooted along Harper Avenue in Yardley.  The power outage set the stage for an apartment building fire.  Thirty-two persons were made homeless in Lower Makefield Township after a candle started a fire.  Bucks County was hardest hit, but 11,500 homes in Montgomery County, 9,600 residences within Philadelphia and 4,700 homes each in Chester and Delaware Counties also lost power.  The latter outages were caused by lightning strikes.  It took nearly 48 hours to restore power to everyone.  Flash flooding in southern Bucks County exacerbated the power problems.

A severe thunderstorm uprooted trees in Pocopson Township.  Squirrels were literally blown out of the trees.  A porch also suffered wind damage.   About 40,000 Philadelphia Electric Company homes lost power in the county mainly around Pocopson and Honey Brook.
Thunderstorm rains caused flooding of streets and low lying areas.
Heavy rains from several thunderstorms produced flooding of streets, low lying areas, basments, and poor drainage areas in a number of locations.
A severe thunderstorm knocked over a few trees and power lines in Gladwyne.

One man was struck by lightning in East Athens. He was working in a barn when lightning struck a nearby house. The lightning traveled through power lines from the house into the barn, striking him and throwing him a distance of 10 to 12 feet.
Branches and a few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds near Bessemer. Some of the trees fell across and downed power lines.
Widespread flooding was reported in and near Uniontown after 2.25 inches of rain fell in one hour. Motorists were stranded and a street collapsed in Uniontown.
Thunderstorm winds downed some trees in Ligonier.
Power lines and trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.
A tree was downed across a road in Plum. Branches were also downed in the area.
A 20 foot by 50 foot section of a roof was torn from a building. Trees and power lines were downed and telephone poles were snapped in New Kensington.
Pitcairn Road was flooded in Monroeville. A rock and mud slide was also reported across a road in Monroeville.
Extensive street flooding was reported in New Kensington.
The low temperature of 37 degrees on May 30 broke the previous record low of 38 degrees, which was set in 1949.

Tropical Storm Bertha passed through Delaware and eastern New Jersey the morning of the 13th.  Wind gusts reached around 40 mph in the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area and around 30 mph in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos.  The strongest winds occurred during the morning of the 13th.  Heavy rain that was falling since the evening of the 12th helped loosen the soil and the winds knocked over weak trees and limbs, mainly around Philadelphia.  One house was heavily damaged in Plymouth Township (Montgomery County) when a 150 foot tree fell on it.  About 60,000 Philadelphia Electric Company customers lost power, mainly in Bucks County.  Heavy rains of between two and four inches fell across the area, with some locally higher amounts in the Poconos.  But since most of the rain fell during a twelve to eighteen hour period and not a shorter duration,  there was some urban and poor drainage flooding, but no major problems.  No serious injuries, water rescues or damage was reported.  Storm totals included 4.4 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 4.01 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 3.87 inches in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 3.56 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 3.1 inches in Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 2.95 inches in Crum Creek (Delaware County), 2.82 inches in Valley Forge (Montgomery County), 2.73 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.63 inches in Jundale (Carbon County), 2.60 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 2.50 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 2.43 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 2.41 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 2.21 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County).
Trees and wires were down in Lewistown, Newton, Hamilton and McVeytown.

Lightning hit the corner of and started a fire in a Moore Township house.  Unfortunately it struck near a propane line that fed the house's furnace.  Fed by the propane, the fire smoldered and damaged the interior walls of the house before it was extinguished.  Damage was estimated at $40,000.
A severe thunderstorm uprooted a couple of trees in northeast Philadelphia.  One landed on and damaged a house.

The first and for many locations the only heat wave of the season occurred during the end of May.  For many locations this heat wave contained the hottest temperatures of the year.  One heat related death occurred within Philadelphia.  A 74-year-old man died on the 21st.  He was found dead in the parking lot at JFK Memorial Hospital.  He was running the car's defroster instead of the air conditioner.  Four other heat related illnesses were reported in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.  In the Lehigh Valley, the Saucon Valley school districts dismissed the children at noon.  In Whitehall Township, Glenside and MacArthur Roads buckled.  In Montgomery County, the Blue Route (Interstate 476) buckled as did United States Route 422 west of Pennsylvania State Route 363.  The excessive heat caused the Pennsylvania New Jersey Interconnection Association (PMJ) of 10 Mid Atlantic Utilities to institute a 5 percent cutback in power usage.  Demand was about 33 percent above normal.  The hottest day was the 20th when records were either tied or set at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports.  Hottest temperatures during this heat wave included 98F at Crum Creek, 97F at Green Land and the Franklin Institute in Philadelphia,  96F at Easton, Hamburg, New Hope, Reading and the  Willow Grove NAS, 94F at East Stroudsburg and 92F at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

Heavy rains caused flooding of small streams and poor drainage areas across a multicounty area of north central Pennsylvania.  Particularly hard hit was the town of Sheffield in Warren County where some evacuations were necessary.
The low temperature of 37 degrees on May 30 broke the previous record low of 38 degrees, which was set in 1949.

An F1 tornado (73 - 112 mph) touched down in the city of Harrisburg demolishing the east side of a truck parts factory along Cameron Street, and peeling the siding off a three story building across the street.  A 16 ton railroad caboose was knocked over by the wind.  The tornado then skipped east for 1/2 mile damaging a second building and downing numerous trees.  Overall damage extended for about 2 miles with a combination of downburst winds and small tornado.
Winds downed trees in Elizabethtown.
Winds took down trees and power lines near Harpers tavern in East Hanover Township.

An F1 tornado (73-112 mph) moved northeast across small towns to the northeast of Pottsville causing damage along a 10 mile path from near New Philadelphia through Brockton and Mary-D before ending at Rabiit Run Reservoir just south of Tamaqua.  Damage occurred along two separate 5 mile segments of the path, running parallel to route 209 from New Philadelphia to just south of Tuscarora on the north side of Sharp Mountain, and from south of Tuscarora to a mile south of Tamaqua on the south side of the mountain.Most of the damage was to trees.  A roof was blown off a home, tiles were removed from a church roof and some siding was torn from a home in Brockton.  A tree fell lengthwise completely demolishing one trailer, and another tree split a second mobile home in two in Mary-D.  Heaviest damage was at Rabbit Run Reservoir where tree tops were torn off.  Fishermen had their cars buried under the debris, but were unhurt.
Winds took the top out of a tree about 3 miles south of Portage.

Heavy rains caused flooding of small streams and poor drainage areas across a multicounty area of north central Pennsylvania.  Particularly hard hit was the town of Sheffield where evacuations were reported.  Roads were also flooded in a number of locations.
Dime size hail fell in Thompsontown and a couple trees were blown over near East Salem.
Heavy rains caused flooding of small streams and poor drainage areas.
Trees were blown down by high winds in Chambersburg.
Thunderstorm winds took down trees and power lines in Blain, West of New Bloombield and near Newport.

Heavy rain and winds associated with thunderstorms caused the collapse of 10 rowhouses within Philadelphia.  No one was injured, but about 20 persons were displaced.  Three houses collapsed on West Montgomery Avenue at 5 p.m. EDT in North Philadelphia.  Two hours later, another two houses collapsed on West Indiana Avenue in North Philadelphia.  Overnight at 230 a.m. EDT two houses were destroyed and three adjoining ones were badly damaged on Woodlane Avenue in the Southwest part of the city.
Large limbs and a few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.
Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Apollo.
Route 66 north and south of Ford City and nearby roads had extensive flooding. Nine basements were also flooded 2 miles south of Ford City.
Strong winds knocked trees down in Liverpool.
The side of a home was damaged by strong winds and trees were knocked down in Enola.
Trees were knocked down by strong winds in the city of York.
Quarter-size hail fell in Hyndman.
Trees were downed onto power lines by strong winds in Auburn.
2-3 inches of rain fell near Tremont causing small stream and poor drainage flooding.
Trees were down on highway between Centerville and Clearville.
Trees were downed by strong winds near McConnelsburg.
.75" hail fell along Rte. 234, 1 mile west of Heidlersburg.
An early morning thunderstorm, that was moving to the northeast at 50 mph, downed a tree and large branches in Penn.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused minor flooding along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek.  Storm totals included 1.55 inches in Honey Brook. A couple of trees were downed by thunderstorm winds across Route 62.

A 53-year-old Plainfield Township woman was struck and injured by lightning as she was walking in her backyard trying to retrieve an American Flag.  Eyewitnesses stated it was not raining at the time she was struck.

A small F1 tornado touched down 5 miles southwest of McKnightstown.  A broken path of damage began near Mt. Carmel Church.  Trees were blown over iin forests and orchards along the path.  Peak winds probably did not exceed 90 mph.

The severe thunderstorm which produced golf-ball sized hail in Chemung and Schuyler counties in New York spawned a tornado as it crossed southeast Bradford County Pennsylvania. Initial damage occurred in Tuscarora Township, where a home received extensive damage. A 25-foot trailer was tossed about one hundred feet, and a garage was completely lifted off its foundation and destroyed. The tornado tracked to the southeast, and moved into Braintrim Township in northwest Wyoming County around 315 PM, then Laceyville where severe tree damage was observed. A barn was destroyed with only one wall left standing. Many trees were sheared off or uprooted in town, and near the Laceyville Bridge as the tornado crossed the Susquehanna River. The tornado then moved into Windham Township at North Flats, along State Route 3001. One eyewitness was a car picked up and spun around, but the occupants were not injured. A mobile home was lifted off its foundation and pushed onto the street. Its occupants were unharmed. The tornado cut across a corn field and sheared off trees within a hedge row as it re-approached the Susquehanna River. Damage was spotty immediately after the tornado crossed the river northwest of Black Walnut. The next area of significant damage occurred about one-quarter mile north of Route 6, on State Route 4009, where many large trees were toppled. The tornado then moved southeast into Black Walnut where many homes were damaged on the north side of Route 6. One mobile home and several garages were completely destroyed. Nearly all of the campsites located south of Route 6, along the northern shore of the Susquehanna River, were damaged by large trees downed by the tornado. The tornado crossed the Susquehanna River once again where it downed and uprooted several hundred trees within a 100 yard swath as it moved up a steep slope. The damage path narrowed near the top of the mountain. It was undetermined whether or not damage occurred downstream from this point, but an aerial survey indicated spotty damage toward the town of Scottsville. The tornado was rated as an F1, with wind speeds between 73 and 122 MPH. The damage path was at least 6 to 7 miles long, and averaged 100 yards in width.As the storm crossed the border into Luzerne County, an F0 tornado touched down in the communities of Duryea and West Avoca. The tornado damaged several homes, with many more homes damaged by uprooted and snapped trees. A portion of these homes had roofing material blown off, gutters and siding separated. The damage path in Luzerne County was broken as the storm skipped to the east/southeast, but extended a total of about one and a half miles.

The severe thunderstorm which produced golf-ball sized hail in Chemung and Schuyler counties in New York spawned a tornado as it crossed southeast Bradford County Pennsylvania. Initial damage occurred in Tuscarora Township, where a home received extensive damage. A 25-foot trailer was tossed about one hundred feet, and a garage was completely lifted off its foundation and destroyed. The tornado tracked to the southeast, and moved into Braintrim Township in northwest Wyoming County around 315 PM, then Laceyville where severe tree damage was observed. A barn was destroyed with only one wall left standing. Many trees were sheared off or uprooted in town, and near the Laceyville Bridge as the tornado crossed the Susquehanna River. The tornado then moved into Windham Township at North Flats, along State Route 3001. One eyewitness was a car picked up and spun around, but the occupants were not injured. A mobile home was lifted off its foundation and pushed onto the street. Its occupants were unharmed. The tornado cut across a corn field and sheared off trees within a hedge row as it re-approached the Susquehanna River. Damage was spotty immediately after the tornado crossed the river northwest of Black Walnut. The next area of significant damage occurred about one-quarter mile north of Route 6, on State Route 4009, where many large trees were toppled. The tornado then moved southeast into Black Walnut where many homes were damaged on the north side of Route 6. One mobile home and several garages were completely destroyed. Nearly all of the campsites located south of Route 6, along the northern shore of the Susquehanna River, were damaged by large trees downed by the tornado. The tornado crossed the Susquehanna River once again where it downed and uprooted several hundred trees within a 100 yard swath as it moved up a steep slope. The damage path narrowed near the top of the mountain. It was undetermined whether or not damage occurred downstream from this point, but an aerial survey indicated spotty damage toward the town of Scottsville. The tornado was rated as an F1, with wind speeds between 73 and 122 MPH. The damage path was at least 6 to 7 miles long, and averaged 100 yards in width.As the storm crossed the border into Luzerne County, an F0 tornado touched down in the communities of Duryea and West Avoca. The tornado damaged several homes, with many more homes damaged by uprooted and snapped trees. A portion of these homes had roofing material blown off, gutters and siding separated. The damage path in Luzerne County was broken as the storm skipped to the east/southeast, but extended a total of about one and a half miles.
Flooding continued in the northern part of the county.

Tropical Storm Bertha passed through Delaware and eastern New Jersey the morning of the 13th.  Wind gusts reached around 40 mph in the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area and around 30 mph in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos.  The strongest winds occurred during the morning of the 13th.  Heavy rain that was falling since the evening of the 12th helped loosen the soil and the winds knocked over weak trees and limbs, mainly around Philadelphia.  One house was heavily damaged in Plymouth Township (Montgomery County) when a 150 foot tree fell on it.  About 60,000 Philadelphia Electric Company customers lost power, mainly in Bucks County.  Heavy rains of between two and four inches fell across the area, with some locally higher amounts in the Poconos.  But since most of the rain fell during a twelve to eighteen hour period and not a shorter duration,  there was some urban and poor drainage flooding, but no major problems.  No serious injuries, water rescues or damage was reported.  Storm totals included 4.4 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 4.01 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 3.87 inches in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 3.56 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 3.1 inches in Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 2.95 inches in Crum Creek (Delaware County), 2.82 inches in Valley Forge (Montgomery County), 2.73 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.63 inches in Jundale (Carbon County), 2.60 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 2.50 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 2.43 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 2.41 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 2.21 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County).
2.8 inches of rain fell in less than an hour near Strasburg causing small stream and poor drainage flooding.

Over 5 inches of rain fell in two hours flooding roads and basements in State College.  The State College High School suffered damage to classrooms on the lowest level and to the swimming pool.  Several labs at Penn State also were damaged.  Between 80 and 100 homes in the area sustained some damage.

A severe thunderstorm uprooted several trees in Phoenixville and caused power outages to about 890 homes and businesses.  Other scattered outages also occurred in Charlestown, East Pikeland, Spring City and West Vincent.

The Little Sandy Creek just northwest of Polk went out of its banks and flooded Route 62 with over 2 feet of water. Flooding also occurred along Sugar Creek, flooding roads near Cooperstown and Route 322, just north of Sugarcreek. Oil Creek also went out of its banks with some road flooding reported near Pioneer.
A SKYWARN spotter reported a wind gust to 70 mph in Honey Brook.
Large hail covered the ground.
Heavy rain caused flooding of streets and low lying areas.  One car was stranded in the high water.
Heavy rain caused flooding of roads and low lying areas.
Heavy rain caused flooding of roads and low lying areas.

Heavy rain caused flooding of streets and low lying areas from Fairview northeast through the city of Erie and Harbor Creek.  In Erie, the Liberty Street underpass, West 12th Street and Route 5 were all closed by the flooding.
Several trees and large branches were downed.
Basements and roads were flooded in upper Yoder and Jackson Township near Johnstown.
Heavy thunderstorms produced flooding on Route 98 north of Crossingville.
Heavy thunderstorms caused flooding of Routes 5 and US 20.
Trees and power lines were brought down in Westmont.
A 60 m.p.h. gust was recorded just south of Johnstown.
Numerous trees were uprooted in the Mt. Hope area due to straight-line winds.  Some minor structural damage was caused by trees falling onto roofs.
Large hail fell in the Mount Davis area.
Trees were downed by strong winds in Chaneysville.
Trees were downed by strong winds in the Everett area.
Trees were downed onto power lines by strong winds in Duncansville.
Severe thunderstorms downed large trees in Scranton and Olyphant.
Part of a roof and the front of a barn was blown off in Charles ville.
Trees were blown down in Altoona.  Many trees were also taken down in Kissler.
1.50 inches of rain was reported in 45 minutes near Slippery Rock. Some nearby roads and low-lying areas were flooded.
Several large trees were downed by thunderstorm winds along Route 30 near Harshaville.
Power lines were downed in Monaca.
Thunderstorm winds tore a section of the roof from a school. Windows were also blown out and nearby trees were downed.
Winds gusted to 65 knots at Penn State University and trees were blown over in Lamont and Park Forest Village.

Chartier's Run and Chartier's Creek went out of their banks. Flooding was reported in Houston and near Westland. State Route 519 was closed due to flood waters, rockslides, and mudslides along the creek. Other minor flooding was reported in the county.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Easton.  It also blew the roof off a water tower.
Extensive street flooding was reported in McDonald.
Power lines were downed in Waynesburg.
Flooding occurred along Pine Run near North Washington. Mobile homes were evacuated and nearby roads were flooded.

Flooding and mudslides were reported in Freedom. Some houses were evacuated. The ground gave way under one house in Center Township. Flooding was reported at Crow's Run in New Sewickley Township and along Elkhorn Run in Monaca. Widespread and prolonged thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced numerous storm total reports of 2 to 3 inches.Along Elkhorn Run, several autos were destroyed by the raging flood waters. Several homes were evacuated. Flood waters washed out a private bridge. Two other county-owned bridges were damaged or destroyed during the flood. A bridge and water main line was washed out in Rochester. Some homes were pushed off their foundations. Mudslides were reported along Route 51 in Monaca and Aliquippa. Numerous roads were flooded and closed. Throughout the county a total of 193 homes and 19 commercial buildings sustained damage, including over $4 million in damages to a shopping center.
Heavy thunderstorm rains flooded Mt. Nebo and Crab Run Roads west of Butler. Small streams, including Crab Run, were also flooded.
A severe thunderstorm uprooted numerous trees in Kidder Township.

A severe thunderstorm brought a swath of large hail to the southern part of Monroe County which reached tea cup size (3 inches) in Gilbert.  Vehicles were dented and windows were broken.  Damaging winds also took down trees and power lines.  In addition to the 3 inch hail in Gilbert, large hail also fell in Brodheadsville (1.75 inches), Stroudsburg (1 inch) and Effort, Saylorsburg and Syndersville (All 3/4 of an inch.).
Large limbs were torn off trees within West Caln Township by a severe thunderstorm.
A severe thunderstorm uprooted trees in Caernarvon Township in extreme southern Berks County.

Thunderstorms dropped between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain over southern sections of Monroe County and caused the flash flooding on some of the smaller streams within the county.  No serious injuries or damage occurred.  Rainfall amounts included 1.65 inches in Long Pond and 3 inches in Saylorsburg.
A few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Delmont.
About 35 trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Penn Hills.
Several large limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds in California.
Lightning sparked a fire at a commercial building.

Heavy rain caused flooding of streets, basements and low lying areas in the cities of Northeast and Erie.  In Erie, the underpass area at 12th and Green Garden was closed due to the flooding and the Erie street department building was flooded.
Heavy rain caused flooding of streets and low lying areas.  Exit seven on I90 was closed.
Heavy rain caused flooding in Conneaut Lake Park and Penn Lakes Village Trailer Park.
Heavy rain caused flooding of roads, basements and low lying areas.  There was one to two feet of water on roads in Millcreek Township.
Several trees and large limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds in swath from near Bolivar to about 3 miles south of New Florence.
Lightning sparked a fire and destroyed a barn.
Heavy rains caused flooding of small streams and poor drainage areas at Beechwood Lake, in Coundersport and other rural areas.
Thunderstorms crossed the region taking down trees in many locations.
Thunderstorm winds took down trees in Penfield.

The first and for many locations the only heat wave of the season occurred during the end of May.  For many locations this heat wave contained the hottest temperatures of the year.  One heat related death occurred within Philadelphia.  A 74-year-old man died on the 21st.  He was found dead in the parking lot at JFK Memorial Hospital.  He was running the car's defroster instead of the air conditioner.  Four other heat related illnesses were reported in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.  In the Lehigh Valley, the Saucon Valley school districts dismissed the children at noon.  In Whitehall Township, Glenside and MacArthur Roads buckled.  In Montgomery County, the Blue Route (Interstate 476) buckled as did United States Route 422 west of Pennsylvania State Route 363.  The excessive heat caused the Pennsylvania New Jersey Interconnection Association (PMJ) of 10 Mid Atlantic Utilities to institute a 5 percent cutback in power usage.  Demand was about 33 percent above normal.  The hottest day was the 20th when records were either tied or set at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports.  Hottest temperatures during this heat wave included 98F at Crum Creek, 97F at Green Land and the Franklin Institute in Philadelphia,  96F at Easton, Hamburg, New Hope, Reading and the  Willow Grove NAS, 94F at East Stroudsburg and 92F at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.
A roof was partially blown off a factory in Seven Valleys.  High winds also ripped shingles from a home in Dallastown.
Trees were downed by thuderstorm winds just north of Warren and at Conowanga.
Winds took down trees on the Allegheny National Forest north of Russell City.
Trees were blown down south of Bradford.
Winds snapped the tops out of trees near Lamar.

Severe thunderstorms caused scattered pockets of wind damage southeast of a line from Breinigsville to Hokendauqua.  A 54-year-old woman was critically injured when a tree fell on her vehicle on Cedar Crest Boulevard in Salisbury Township.  In South Whitehall Township, downed trees blocked six roads.  Trees damaged a house in Hokendauqua and took down a fence in Wescosville.   About 49,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light Customers lost power during the storm in their 29 county service area that includes parts of the Lehigh Valley.  As of 9 p.m. EDT that evening, 27,000 still were without power.  Power was not completely restored until Monday the 13th.
A severe thunderstorm uprooted trees in Gilbert.
Lightning started a fire and damaged the third floor balcony of an apartment building within Reading.
Yankee Creek and Little Neshannock Creek went out of their banks and flooded nearby roads.

Severe thunderstorms caused wind damage across eastern parts of Northamton County.  Wind damage was reported in Wilson Borough, Hellertown, Easton, Williams Township, Raubsville, Upper Mount Bethel Township and Bangor.  Downed trees forced road closures in Easton.  A car was crushed by a tree in Wilson Borough.  About 1,750 homes in the county lost power.  No injuries were reported.

Thunderstorm winds downed a tree and large branches in Marienville.
A couple of trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.
Thunderstorm winds downed a couple of trees along Banksville Road in Dormont.
Severe thunderstorms produced quarter-sized hail 4 miles northeast of Troy, and nickel-sized hail in Troy. The hail covered the ground in both locations.
Lightning struck and damaged the roof of a West Rockhill Township house.

Nearly stationary thunderstorms brought three to eleven and a half inches of rain within a four hour period to southeast Bucks County.  The 11.55 inches of rain that fell in Langhorne represented a greater than one hundred year single day storm.  Two men drowned, a 54-year-old and a 52-year-old.  Damage was estimated at 14.5 million dollars, 13 million to property and 1.5 million to state and local roads.  A state of emergency was declared that evening.  The county was declared a major disaster area later in the month.  Ninety people escaped possible injury by evacuating Gold's Gym in Middletown Township just before the roof collapsed from the heavy rain at 610 p.m. EDT.   Nearly every major roadway was made impassable by the torrential downpours including Pennsylvania State Route 413 and U.S. Routes 1 and 13.  Even Interstate 95 was closed at 8 p.m. EDT in Penndel Borough.  Creeks that flooded included the Neshaminy, Brock, Mill, Queen Anne, Indian, Silver and Deer Run. The hardest hit townships were Middletown ($11.5 million in damage), Falls, Lower Makefield and Bristol.  The hardest hit boroughs within these towns were Bristol, Langhorne, Morrisville, Penndel and Yardley.  Seven bridges were washed out.  Four homes were destroyed, 62 suffered major damage, 215 had moderate damage and 381 incurred minor damage.  About 30 businesses were also damaged including a bowling alley in Langhorne that was ruined. The first fatality occurred about 8 p.m. EDT.  A 52 year-old-man on his way to his daughter's cancelled graduation drove his car into the Silver Creek.  The vehicle plunged about 15 feet into the swollen creek.  His body was found near the 15th hole of the adjacent Yardley Country Club.  Before he arrived, the creek had washed out the single lane 107-year-old bridge on Reading Avenue.  Ironically another woman was probably saved because her vehicle  landed on top of his.  She managed to get out of her car and clinged to a tree until she was rescued.  The second fatality occurred about 1230 a.m. EDT on the 13th in Penn Valley/Falls Township.  A 54-year-old man's vehicle stalled in four feet of water on Newbold Road near the Penn Warner Industrial Park.  He rescued his mother from the flood waters, but returned to his car.  He was then plowed over by a wall of water from a passing truck.  His body was found near his vehicle's bumper.Middletown Township had the most flood damage.  The worst was along the Mill Creek and on Wyoming Avenue.  In the Penndel Borough dozens of people were trapped on the second floor of their homes on Wyoming and Delaware Avenues.   Two hundred shoppers at the Oxford Valley mall were stranded until about midnight EDT.  Flooding along the Mill Creek continued through Bristol Township.   Boats were used to rescue stranded motorists.  The Bristol Senior Citizen Center was submerged in four feet of water.  Four bridges were washed out in Falls Township and emergency personnel waded in four feet of water to rescue stranded motorists.  In Lower Makefield Township, the flooding washed out the SEPTA train tracks.  Cars were pushed out of the driveways onto the streets by the flood waters.  Three bridges were washed away.  In Yardley Borough, flooding along the Brock and Silver Creeks and the Canal inundated the area again for the second time this year.  About 200 to 300 persons were rescued.  Cars were buried in water.  Evacuations were ordered between the Canal and Delaware River.  Officials feared gas explosions from eroded roadways and exposed lines.  Power was not restored until Friday the 14th.  In Bensalem, the Neshaminy Creek flooded.  A 40 foot boat and its dock were ripped from their moorings and smashed into the U.S. Route 13 Bridge.  Farther upstream the Neshaminy Creek in Langhorne crested at 11.8 feet at 115 a.m. EDT.  Flood stage is 9 feet.              In addition to the 11.55 inches of rain that fell near the Oxford Valley Mall, other reported rainfall amounts included 9 inches at the Middletown Country Club, 6.1 inches in Yardley, 5 inches in Neshaminy Falls, 4.8 inches in Richboro and 3.3 inches in Southampton.
Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.
Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.
Power lines and trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Indiana.

Two to three feet of water covered some streets in Indiana. Up to three feet of water made Route 119 3 miles north of Indiana impassable. Marsh Run in Indiana went over its banks and flooded nearby roads.

Six people were injured, none seriously, after lightning struck close by during the Westmoreland County Air Show. Four of the injured were sent to an area hospital and were treated and released. All four complained of numbness in their arms and legs.
A severe thunderstorm uprooted a few trees in Haverford Township.
Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Ramsaytown.
Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds across Moore Bridge Road, west of Richardsville.
Lightning struck a house in Greensburg and sparked a small attic fire.

A severe thunderstorm uprooted trees and tossed lawn furniture in Horsham.  One house was damaged by a tree in the Hidden Creek Development as a unearthed willow tree punctured a hole in the roof.  About 2 miles northeast of Horsham the same severe thunderstorm uprooted two large oak trees and ripped the siding from one house.
Golf ball size hail fell in New Hope.  Motorists pulled underneath overpasses to avoid hail damage to their vehicles.
A late season snowfall blanketed the northern tier of Pennsylvania. Accumulations ranged from about an inch in the valleys, to as much as 3 inches in the higher elevations.
A SKYWARN Spotter reported a wind gust to 63 mph in Levittown.
Trees were blown down in Farmers Valley.  A window was blown out of a house in Warren where pea size hail was also reported.
Trees were downed by strong winds along Rte. 74 in Saville Township.
Strong winds brought power lines down in Windber.
Dime-size hail fell in Bedford.

Power lines were brought down onto a house by strong winds in Windber.
Power lines downed in Potters Mills.
Trees were downed by strong winds along Rte. 422, 2 miles west of Revloc.
Quarter-size hail fell along Rte. 234, 1 mile west of Heidlersburg.
Golf-ball size hail fell in Dillsburg.
A tree was brought down by strong winds in Southmont.
Strong winds brought trees down onto a house in Hughesville.

Major flooding occurred in Gettysburg.  Thunderstorms developed northeast parts of Adams County and continued to develop southwest into Gettysburg as the evening progressed.  More than 9 inches of rain from 9:00 p.m. to 1:00 a.m. in Gettysburg with a storm total in excess of 11 inches.  Damage estimates were around $5 million in the borough and surrounding townships.  50 homes and 19 businesses, 8 bridges and many roads and streets were damaged.  Two teenage girls were swept away in the waters of Stevens Run, but were rescued by firemen.  The Sach's Mill Covered Bridge across Marsh Creek which predated the Civil War was washed off its piers and lay crumpled on the stream bank.  At one point every major road leading into Gettysburg was closed.
Evacuations occurred along Conewago Creek as runoff from heavy rains in Adams County moved downstream.
Severe thunderstorms downed trees onto power lines along Route 191 in Dyberry Township.
A tree, some large branches and power lines were downed by thunderstorm winds.
Heavy rains flooded basements in Upper Yoder Township near Johnstown.  Route 56 was under 5 to 6 feet of water.
Strong winds brought a tree down on Moonlight Road in Conemaugh Township.
Strong winds brought trees down onto power lines in Blue Ridge Summit.
Large limbs were downed by strong winds in Mercersburg.
Trees and power lines were downed by strong winds in Mill Hall.
Small streams and roads were flooded in Shirleysburg and other areas.
Strong winds knocked trees down along Rte. 80 near West Buffalo Township.

Major flooding occurred as thunderstorms stalled and dumped torrential rains across the St. Thomas area west of Chambersburg.  Up to 12 inches of rain fell in the area overnight, washing out roads and flooding homes.  A 42 year old female drowned after driving into a flooded road at 11:45 p.m.  Rescue attempts failed.  Damage estimates were in the range of $1 million.
Two roads were closed and small streams were out of banks across southern Huntingdon County.
Trees and power lines were knocked down by strong winds in Anthony Township.
Road flooding occurred along Route 522 about 5 miles south of McConnellsburg.

Trees were knocked down in the Harrisburg area due to strong winds.

Severe thunderstorms downed several trees in Oakland.

Trees downed power lines due to strong winds near Mexico, in the town of Center.

Strong winds knocked trees down and blew limbs onto power lines in the Harrisburg area and Swatara Township.
Basements were flooded and heavy rains caused mudslides near Columbia.

Harrisburg International Airport reported a gust to 60 knots.

Severe thunderstorms caused wind damage in a number of towns in the eastern third of Berks County.   Damage was reported in Mount Penn, Pennside, Exeter, Kutztown, Topton and Boyertown. .75" hail fell southwest of Biglerville.

Trees were downed and a roof was blown off a schoolhouse near Gettysburg.  A silo top was also blown off.
Strong winds brought trees down onto power lines in Mercersburg.
Strong winds brought a tree down at Rte. 234 and Old Carlisle Road in Biglerville.
Dime-size hail fell in New Oxford for fifteen minutes.
Strong winds brought trees and power lines down in Hanover.
Trees were downed by strong winds in Mechanicsburg.
Large limbs were knocked down by strong winds in Haneyville.
Dime-size hail fell in Harrison Valley.

The severe thunderstorm which produced golf-ball sized hail in Chemung and Schuyler counties in New York spawned a tornado as it crossed southeast Bradford County Pennsylvania. Initial damage occurred in Tuscarora Township, where a home received extensive damage. A 25-foot trailer was tossed about one hundred feet, and a garage was completely lifted off its foundation and destroyed. The tornado tracked to the southeast, and moved into Braintrim Township in northwest Wyoming County around 315 PM, then Laceyville where severe tree damage was observed. A barn was destroyed with only one wall left standing. Many trees were sheared off or uprooted in town, and near the Laceyville Bridge as the tornado crossed the Susquehanna River. The tornado then moved into Windham Township at North Flats, along State Route 3001. One eyewitness was a car picked up and spun around, but the occupants were not injured. A mobile home was lifted off its foundation and pushed onto the street. Its occupants were unharmed. The tornado cut across a corn field and sheared off trees within a hedge row as it re-approached the Susquehanna River. Damage was spotty immediately after the tornado crossed the river northwest of Black Walnut. The next area of significant damage occurred about one-quarter mile north of Route 6, on State Route 4009, where many large trees were toppled. The tornado then moved southeast into Black Walnut where many homes were damaged on the north side of Route 6. One mobile home and several garages were completely destroyed. Nearly all of the campsites located south of Route 6, along the northern shore of the Susquehanna River, were damaged by large trees downed by the tornado. The tornado crossed the Susquehanna River once again where it downed and uprooted several hundred trees within a 100 yard swath as it moved up a steep slope. The damage path narrowed near the top of the mountain. It was undetermined whether or not damage occurred downstream from this point, but an aerial survey indicated spotty damage toward the town of Scottsville. The tornado was rated as an F1, with wind speeds between 73 and 122 MPH. The damage path was at least 6 to 7 miles long, and averaged 100 yards in width.As the storm crossed the border into Luzerne County, an F0 tornado touched down in the communities of Duryea and West Avoca. The tornado damaged several homes, with many more homes damaged by uprooted and snapped trees. A portion of these homes had roofing material blown off, gutters and siding separated. The damage path in Luzerne County was broken as the storm skipped to the east/southeast, but extended a total of about one and a half miles.

Widespread flash flooding was reported in Pittsburgh and throughout Allegheny County. Heavy thunderstorm rains dropped 1.62 inches of rain in 45 minutes at the National Weather Service Office in Moon Township. Runoff from the rain flooded many streets in Moon Township. Thorn Run overflowed its banks and damaged a pool and swept mud and water into dozens of homes. The creek washed out a foot bridge and tore down a 50 foot retaining wall. Flaugherty Run in Moon Township went out of its banks and flooded nearby roads. A lobby of a hotel in the township was also flooded. Route 8 north of Etna was closed due to flood waters. Roads were also flooded in Imperial. Mudslides were reported along Allegheny River Boulevard near Nadine. Flash flooding occurred along Little Sewickley Creek. Grant Street in Pittsburgh was flooded. Several roads were flooded in Millvale and mudslides were reported in Aspinwal. Route 60 was flooded along McClaren's Run. A creek was out of its banks and flooded Hookstown Grade Road. Street flooding was also reported in Leetsdale and Sewickley. A shopping center was flooded in Leetsdale. Mount Nebo Road and Camp Horne Road was closed due to high flood waters.The Pittsburgh International Airport received a total of 3.11 inches of rain on the 24th, a new daily record. Also, this amount was the second greatest daily rainfall total ever recorded in the month of June, based on precipitation records dating back to 1872.One-half of the total (1.55 inches) fell in just 29 minutes.
Several roads were flooded in Monaca.
A house was struck by lightning. A resulting fire caused damage to the roof.

The first and for many locations the only heat wave of the season occurred during the end of May.  For many locations this heat wave contained the hottest temperatures of the year.  One heat related death occurred within Philadelphia.  A 74-year-old man died on the 21st.  He was found dead in the parking lot at JFK Memorial Hospital.  He was running the car's defroster instead of the air conditioner.  Four other heat related illnesses were reported in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.  In the Lehigh Valley, the Saucon Valley school districts dismissed the children at noon.  In Whitehall Township, Glenside and MacArthur Roads buckled.  In Montgomery County, the Blue Route (Interstate 476) buckled as did United States Route 422 west of Pennsylvania State Route 363.  The excessive heat caused the Pennsylvania New Jersey Interconnection Association (PMJ) of 10 Mid Atlantic Utilities to institute a 5 percent cutback in power usage.  Demand was about 33 percent above normal.  The hottest day was the 20th when records were either tied or set at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports.  Hottest temperatures during this heat wave included 98F at Crum Creek, 97F at Green Land and the Franklin Institute in Philadelphia,  96F at Easton, Hamburg, New Hope, Reading and the  Willow Grove NAS, 94F at East Stroudsburg and 92F at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.
A severe thunderstorm took down some power lines in north central Berks County in Centre, Maiden Creek and Ontelaunee Townships.  About 1,500 homes lost power.  Large hail also fell in Penn Township.
A number of roads were closed in the northwest part of the county. Small streams and roads were also flooded near Lynch.
Cherry Run came out of its banks and caused flooding just south of Langville.
Thunderstorm winds took down trees in Clearfield and Osceloa Mills.  Trees were also blown down in Johnstown.
Heavy rain caused flooding of streets, basements and low lying areas.  One street was cut in half by a cave-in.  Cars were stranded and some homes were evacuated.
Trees and powerlines were taken down between English Center and Buttonwood.
Heavy rains caused small streams to overflow their banks.
Trees fell on a car and a home in Lewisburg

Several small streams and roads were flooded from heavy rain throughout the county. The flooding was most notable in the northern part of the county. Nashua Road near Nashua was flooded. A few cottages along the Little Neshanock Creek sustained damage from flood waters. Roads were also closed in low-lying areas along the Shenango River.
Trees and powerlines were blown down by thunderstorm winds.  Winds gusted to 60 mph in Millville.
The roof of a building was lifted and about 15 autos were damaged in a parking lot in Sunbury as a thunderstorm moved through the area.
Winds took down trees and power lines across the central part of the county from Johnstown to Ebensburg and Cresson.
Winds blew down trees and powerlines in Somerset and Jennerstown.
A roof was blown off a business near New Holland.
Winds blew down a tree 1 miles east of Millersburg.

Rain, which fell from December 1st into the morning of the 2nd, became heavy at times during the early morning hours on the second.  This caused flooding on some of the smaller creeks and streams as well as urban flooding in Chester County.  At the Philadelphia International Airport, 0.56 inches of rain fell within an hour between 2 a.m. and 3 a.m. EST on the 2nd.  The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 5 a.m. through 830 a.m. EST on the 2nd.  It crested at 7.2 feet at 630 a.m. EST on the 2nd.  Farther downstream along the main stem of the Brandywine, the intersection of Pennsylvania State Routes 100 and 926 along the Birmingham/Pocopson Townships border was closed due to the creek's flooding.  During the late afternoon and early evening of the 2nd, some flooding occurred along the Schuylkill River.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.  Storm totals included 2.52 inches in Honeybrook and 2.22 inches in Glenmoore.

6-10 inches of snow fell across Somerset, Cambria, and western Blair Counties closing Rt. 350 between Phillipsburg and the Bald Eagle intersection. 6.8 inches of snow fell on northern Schuylkill and 5.8 inches fell on Harrisburg, breaking the snowfall record for the date.

The year 1996 was one of the wettest ones on record for Eastern Pennsylvania.  Precipitation amounts on a county average ranged from 59.5 inches in Lehigh County to a whopping 72.9 inches in Monroe County.  This was about 14 to 26 inches above normal.  It was the wettest year on record in Philadelphia as 56.47 inches of precipitation fell at the International Airport breaking the previous record of 55.28 inches set in 1973.  It also was the wettest year on record in Stroudsburg.  Other yearly precipitation totals included 74.45 inches in Exton (Chester County), 71.49 inches in Long Pond (Monroe County), 67.88 inches at Springtown (Bucks County), 63.96 inches in Spring House (Montgomery County) and 56.87 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.
A wind gust of 60 miles per hour was reported.
Large limbs were downed.

Heavy lake effect snow accumulation ranged mostly from six to seven inches, although Edinboro reported 11 inches.  Corry, Cranesville,  Franklin Center and the city of Erie received six inches, while Hatch Hollow and Union City each received seven inches.

The combination of some very light snow (for most the first snow of the season), road and air temperatures below freezing and heavy  traffic volume because of Thanksgiving Day caused roadways to become extremely slippery and hazardous, especially on bridges and overpasses.  Accumulations were less than an inch.  But that was enough to cause numerous accidents across the area.  Because of the holiday and the surprising roadway result of this light snow, it took a while for road crews to recover. Although the snow ended by 6 p.m. EDT, road conditions did not improve in some areas until 10 p.m EST. In Philadelphia, a 30-year-old woman died after losing control of her car and slammed into a tree. The Platt and Grays Ferry Bridges were closed from late afternoon until 9 p.m. EST.  By 5 p.m. EST all the major arteries within the city had slowed to a crawl.  In Chester County, two people were seriously injured in a four vehicle collision on a Pennsylvania State Route 100 bridge over the Schuylkill River.  The U.S. Route 30 Bypass had many accidents.  In West Goshen alone, emergency personnel responded to 43 accidents.  The Tredyffrin Police had to close the southbound lanes of U.S. Route 202.  In Delaware County, the Commodore Barry Bridge was closed due to icing until 9 p.m. EST.Pennsylvania State Route 309 was very icy in Bucks and Montgomery Counties.  Elsewhere in Montgomery County, portions of the Blue Route (Interstate 476) were closed as were the West Chester Pike over the Blue Route and U.S. Route 202 over the Dannehower Bridge.  Many accidents were reported on Pennsylvania State Route 29 from Schwenksville to Collegeville and along Germantown Pike and U.S. Route 422.In the Lehigh Valley, the worst problems were in Northampton County.  Travel was extremely treacherous along U.S. Route 22, Pennsylvania State Route 512 and over the Glendon Bridge.

A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front and moved rapidly across Bradford county.  These storms produced very strong wind gusts which resulted in downed trees and power lines in and around East Smithfield.

The line of severe thunderstorms that produced damage in Bradford county moved eastward into Susquehanna county.  Several large trees were blown down with some tree tops snapped off in and just west of Montrose.
Heavy rains caused small stream flooding throughout Lancaster County.
A severe thunderstorm snapped off a six-foot diameter tree in Warrington and damaged a chimney.  Several smaller trees were also snapped off.  Outdoor furniture was blown off a deck.
A few basements were flooded near Pulaski.

A slow moving cold front and a low pressure system that developed in West Virginia on the first and moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 2nd caused heavy rain and flooding along the Lehigh and Schuylkill Rivers.  The heaviest rain fell during the early morning on the second.  Storm totals averaged two to three inches, with some higher amounts in the Poconos.The Lehigh River at Walnutport was above its 8 foot flood stage from 430 a.m. until 5 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  It crested at 9.5 feet at 9 a.m. EST on the second.  Minor flooding occurred along the Schuylkill River from Berne through Pottstown.  In Berne, the river reached its 12 foot flood stage at 3 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 12.9 feet at 745 a.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 1 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  In Reading, the river was above its 13 foot flood stage from 1130 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 830 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  It crested at 13.7 feet at 430 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  In Pottstown, the river reached its 13 foot flood stage at 5 p.m. EST on the 17th, crested at 13.1 feet at 7 p.m. EST on the 17th and was back within bankfull at 9 p.m. EST.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.Storm precipitation totals included 3.5 inches in Reading, 2.74 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 2.52 inches in Honey Brook (Chester County), 2.5 inches in Vinemont (Berks County), 2.28 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.22 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County) 2.07 inches in Palm (Montgomery County), 1.98 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County) and 1.89 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.The following day (December 3rd) a water rescue was performed along the Maiden Creek along Pennsylvania State Route 143 in Greenwich Township in Berks County.  Two men were canoeing on the Maiden Creek when it capsized.  One swam to safety, while the other clung to a tree for over an hour before being rescued.  The creek was within bankfull,  but the recent heavy rain had made it rougher and swelled its stage to about four feet above normal.
Lake effect snow squalls accumulated 6 inches at Union City and 7 inches at Corry.

Rain, which fell from December 1st into the morning of the 2nd, became heavy at times during the early morning of the 2nd.  At the Philadelphia International Airport, 0.56 inches of rain fell between 2 a.m. and 3 a.m EST on the 2nd.  The heavy rain caused flooding on some of the smaller streams as well as urban flooding in Bucks County.  The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne, reached its 9 foot flood stage at 530 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 10.7 feet at 1 p.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 430 p.m. EST on the 2nd. Storm totals included 3.24 inches in Doylestown, 2.87 inches in Neshaminy Falls, 2.74 inches in Springtown, 2.3 inches in Southampton, 2.28 inches in Perkasie, 2.2 inches in Fricks, 2.18 inches in Sellersville, and 1.94 inches in New Hope.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

Rain, which fell from December 1st into the morning of the 2nd became heavy at times during the early morning of the second.  At the Philadelphia International Airport, 0.56 inches of rain fell between 2 a.m. and 3 a.m. EST.  The heavy rain caused urban flooding as well as flooding along the Frankford Creek.  At the Castor Avenue gage, the creek crested at 6.2 feet at 245 a.m. EST.  Flood stage is estimated at 5 feet.  Storm totals included 1.98 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 1.78 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina the afternoon of the 5th.  As it quickly intensified and moved northeast (It passed just east of the New Jersey coast the morning of the 6th.), the precipitation associated with it changed from rain to snow across the higher terrain of the Poconos the evening of the 5th and over the higher terrain of northeast Berks County during the early morning of the 6th.  As the storm intensified during the early morning hours of the 6th, so did the precipitation.  The heaviest snow fell between about 2 a.m. and 8 a.m EST.  The snow also was very elevation dependent.  While heavy snow fell over the higher terrain, mainly just rain fell in Reading.  A mixture of rain and snow fell in Stroudsburg where less than one inch of snow accumulated.Snowfall accumulations in Monroe County reached 19 inches in Tobyhanna and 15 inches in Mount Pocono.  The Mount Pocono borough had to declare a state of emergency after the heavy snow and jacknifed tractor trailers forced the closures of Interstates 80 and 380.  Traffic was diverted through the borough on Pennsylvania State Routes 611 and 940.  Dozens of weather related accidents were reported by state police throughout the Poconos.  Other snowfall accumulations included 13 inches in Long Pond, 11 inches in Pocono Lakes and 8 inches in Jundale in Carbon County.  The snow was heavy and very wet and pulled down numerous power lines.  About 38,000 homes and businesses in Pennsylvania, Power and Light's service area in Northeast Pennsylvania lost power with Monroe and Wayne Counties hit the hardest.  The hardest hit locations within Monroe County were Blakeslee, Mount Pocono and Mountainhome.  There were still 800 homes without power as of 5 p.m. EST on the 7th.  In Carbon County, classes were cancelled in the eastern part of the county in Penn Forest and Kidder Townships.    In Berks County, six to eight inches of snow fell over the higher terrain of the eastern part of the county from Fleetwood to Hereford Township.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey coast during the evening of the 7th and near Portland Maine on the morning of the 8th.  In a similar occurrence to what transpired during the morning of the 6th, precipitation spread across the Poconos by noon on the 7th and precipitation type became elevation dependent.  While a mixture of rain and snow fell in the valleys, more heavy wet snow fell over the higher terrain.  The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening on the 7th.  Snowfall accumulations averaged about an inch or two in the valleys (including East Stroudsburg and Stroudsburg) to around 6 inches over the higher terrain in Northern Carbon and Monroe Counties.  Numerous accidents were reported in Coolbaugh, Mount Pocono, Tobyhanna and Tunkhannock Townships.  The Pocono Mountain Regional Police requested that all motorists stay off the roads.  Numerous accidents were also reported in Kidder, Nesquehoning and Weatherly Townships in Carbon County.

Heavy rain associated with a slow moving low pressure system near Delaware the night of the 13th helped cause flooding across some of the larger streams and rivers in Southeast Pennsylvania.  At the Philadelphia International Airport 1.84 inches of rain fell during the 12 hour period ending 5 a.m. EST on the 14th.  This heavy rain was part of a continuous 48 hour stretch of rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th that dropped 2.5 to 4.5 inches across most of Southeast Pennsylvania.In Chester County, the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage through 11 a.m. EST on the 14th.  It crested at 10.1 feet at 4.a.m. EST on the 14th.  The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford reached its 9 foot flood stage at 130 a.m. EST on the 14th, crested at 11.1 feet at 1130 a.m. EST on the 14th and was back within bankfull at 10 p.m. EST on the 14th.  Some motorists became trapped in the flood waters at the Pennsylvania State Routes 926 and 100 intersection along the Pocopson and Birmingham Townships border.  In Delaware County, the Chester Creek near Chester was above its 8 foot flood stage from 230 a.m. through 6 a.m. EST on the 14th.  It crested at 8.5 feet at 4 a.m. EST on the 14th.  Storm totals included 3.33 inches in Downingtown, 3.31 inches in Glenmoore, 3.27 in Crum Creek and 2.89 inches in Honey Brook.In Philadelphia, the Schuylkill River at the Fairmount Dam was above its 11 foot flood stage from 7 a.m. through 330 p.m. EST on the 14th.  It crested at 11.4 feet at 1030 a.m. EST on the 14th. Kelly Drive was closed because of the flooding and the Crescent Club Boathouse was partially submerged.  Storm totals included 4.17 inches at the Franklin Institute, 3.81 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 3.45 inches in Center City and 3.37 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.In Montgomery County, the Perkiomen Creek at Graterford was above its 11 foot flood stage from 1 a.m. through 10 a.m EST on the 14th.  It crested at 12.0 feet at 615 a.m. EST on the 14th.  Several roads had to be closed during the night of the 13th.  Hardest hit was Horsham Township.  In Bucks County, the Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne reached its 9 foot flood stage at 3 a.m. EST on the 14th, crested at 12.6 feet at noon EST on the 14th and was back within bankfull at 730 p.m. EST on the 14th.  Six homes were evacuated because of flooding along the Neshaminy Creek in Middletown Township.  Cars became stranded because of the creek's flooding in Middletown and Lower Southampton. Basement flooding was reported in Hulmeville and several roads were closed in Bristol.  Storm totals included 5.05 inches at the Willow Grove NAS, 4.7 inches in Neshaminy Falls, 3.66 inches in Southampton, 3.3 inches in Doylestown, 3.08 inches in Valley Forge, 2.77 inches in Perkasie, 2.61 inches in Green Lane and 1.76 inches in Palm.

A slow moving cold front and a low pressure system that developed in West Virginia on the first and moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 2nd caused heavy rain and flooding along the Lehigh and Schuylkill Rivers.  The heaviest rain fell during the early morning on the second.  Storm totals averaged two to three inches, with some higher amounts in the Poconos.The Lehigh River at Walnutport was above its 8 foot flood stage from 430 a.m. until 5 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  It crested at 9.5 feet at 9 a.m. EST on the second.  Minor flooding occurred along the Schuylkill River from Berne through Pottstown.  In Berne, the river reached its 12 foot flood stage at 3 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 12.9 feet at 745 a.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 1 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  In Reading, the river was above its 13 foot flood stage from 1130 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 830 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  It crested at 13.7 feet at 430 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  In Pottstown, the river reached its 13 foot flood stage at 5 p.m. EST on the 17th, crested at 13.1 feet at 7 p.m. EST on the 17th and was back within bankfull at 9 p.m. EST.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.Storm precipitation totals included 3.5 inches in Reading, 2.74 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 2.52 inches in Honey Brook (Chester County), 2.5 inches in Vinemont (Berks County), 2.28 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.22 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County) 2.07 inches in Palm (Montgomery County), 1.98 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County) and 1.89 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.The following day (December 3rd) a water rescue was performed along the Maiden Creek along Pennsylvania State Route 143 in Greenwich Township in Berks County.  Two men were canoeing on the Maiden Creek when it capsized.  One swam to safety, while the other clung to a tree for over an hour before being rescued.  The creek was within bankfull,  but the recent heavy rain had made it rougher and swelled its stage to about four feet above normal.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th and then drifted southeast to about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  A prolonged period of rain and snow fell across the area.  As precipitation increased in intensity, it changed over to heavy snow during the evening of the 13th and lasted through the morning of the 14th.  Accumulations were as high as six inches in Carbon County and ranged from three inches in the valleys to close to a foot over the higher terrain in Monroe County.Once again Monroe County was hit the hardest as heavy wet snow took down trees and power lines.  About 6,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers lost power in Brodheadsville, Henryville, Lake Naomi and Stroudsburg.  About 5,000 General Public Utilities customers lost power in Cherry Valley, Delaware Water Gap, Saylorsburg, Stroud Township and Syndersville.  Full power was not restored until the 15th.  Many homes were covered with fallen limbs and a fallen tree destroyed one garage in Shawnee.  Downed trees blocked roads including West Main Street in Stroudsburg, U.S. Route 209 in Smithfield Township and Pennsylvania State Route 191 near Cherry Hill.Snowfall accumulations included 11 inches in Long Pond, 7 inches in Saylorsburg, 5 inches in Jundale and 3.5 inches in Stroudsburg.  The combination of snow and rain dropped about two to 3.5 inches of precipitation over a 48 hour period in the area.  Precipitation totals included 3.32 inches in East Stroudsburg and 1.91 inches in Jundale. To date Stroudsburg broke its all-time yearly precipitation record amassing 68.03 inches so far in 1996.  The previous record was 65.83 inches.

December 1996 was one of the wettest Decembers on record for Eastern Pennsylvania.  Precipitation amounts ranged from about 4 inches above normal in Berks County to around 6.5 inches above normal in both Delaware and Monroe Counties.  A new December precipitation record was set at the Philadelphia International Airport where 8.48 inches fell.  The previous record was 7.37 inches set in 1983.  December 1996 was the second wettest December on record at the Lehigh Valley International Airport where 7.3 inches of precipitation fell.  The record remains 7.89 inches set in 1973.  County average monthly precipitation totals ranged from 7.6 inches in Berks County to 10.3 inches in Monroe County.

Damaging southwest winds moved through Berks County and the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon of the 1st.In Berks County, about 15,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light homes in the southwest part of the county lost power.  Spring Township was the hardest hit community with numerous downed trees reported.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured in Vinemont at 4 p.m. EST.  Other communities losing power included Robesonia, Shillington, Sinking Spring, Womelsdorf and Wyomissing.  A wind gust to 58 mph was measured at the Reading Regional Airport.  Dozens of trees were uprooted within Reading.  In West Reading at 5 p.m. EST the wind peeled the roof from a rowhouse off and damaged another home's chimney.  In Robeson Township, the wind peeled a 50-foot-long roof from a machinery shed near the Green Hills Golf Course.  Another 2,600 General Power Utilities homes also lost power in the eastern part of the county around 4 p.m. EST in Birdsboro, Boyertown, Flying Hills and Topton.  Eighty percent of the customers had power returned by 9 p.m. EST.  A few trees were also uprooted in Bethel Township.       In Lehigh County, several trees were knocked down in Coopersburg near the Pennsylvania State Route 519 Junction.  In Northampton County, about 1,500 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers lost power in Hanover and Bethlehem.  Downed trees blocked roadways in Easton and Hanover.  Trees also littered Pennsylvania State Route 611 north of Easton.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina the afternoon of the 5th.  As it quickly intensified and moved northeast (It passed just east of the New Jersey coast the morning of the 6th.), heavy rain spread across Southeast Pennsylvania after midnight on the 6th.  Storm totals averaged between 1.00 and 1.50 inches across the area, the second soaking precipitation event of the month. Precipitation changed to snow before ending across the area. Accumulations (Mostly an inch or two) were confined to the Lehigh Valley and the far northern and western suburbs of Philadelphia.  Precipitation totals included 1.82 inches (2.0 inches of snow) in Palm (Montgomery County), 1.58 inches in Crum Creek (Delaware County), 1.4 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 1.38 inches in Phoenixville (Chester County), 1.37 inches at the Willow Grove NAS, 1.32 inches (1.5 inches of snow) in Springtown (Bucks County), 1.31 inches (1.0 inch of snow) at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.17 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.
Strong winds, ahead of an intense, fast moving cold front, gusted to 58 miles per hour in Sewickley Heights.

A tornado, which had a damage path about 10 miles long, moved across the southwest part of the county from near Kunkletown in Eldred Township to near Syndersville in Hamilton Township.  The tornado was not continuously on the ground during its entire lifetime, but did cause wind damage in four townships: Eldred, Ross, southeastern Chestnut Hill and Hamilton.  The touch downs were described as brief, but numerous.  About 15 homes and a hanger were damaged with numerous trees uprooted.  The worst damage occurred around Green View Avenue in northwestern Hamilton Township and around Kunkletown Road in Ross Township.  The damage estimate was around $400,000.  The tornado strength was estimated as F1 (Winds 73 to 112 mph) on the Fujita Scale.The tornado initially touched down around 350 p.m. near Kunkletown in Eldred Township.  Five homes were damaged within the township.  Farther northeast in Ross Township along Kunkletown Road, five homes including an unoccupied trailer sustained major damage.  In Hamilton Township, the tornado blew the roof off one home and threw it 100 yards away; a closed-in porch from another home imploded and an airplane hanger and several small planes within it were damaged.  A tree also fell on and damaged an unoccupied school bus.  About 3,000 homes mainly within Ross and Hamilton Townships lost power.

In addition to the wind damage caused by the tornado, severe thunderstorms within the same thunderstorm complex and on both sides of the tornado caused wind damage in Brodheadsville and Saylorsburg.  In Brodheadsville, trees were uprooted.  In Saylorsburg, in addition to the downed trees and wires, shingles were torn from the roof of several homes.

The same thunderstorm complex that produced the tornado in the southwestern part of Monroe County, produced wind damage in the eastern part of the county in Stroud and Price Townships.  Numerous trees were reported down.  The worst damage reported in Stroud Township occurred around the Beacon Hill area, while the worst damage in Price Township was along Manzanedo Drive.  Within Price Township the driver of a van was seriously injured when a tree fell on the vehicle and pinned him in on Pennsylvania State Route 447.  The passenger escaped with just minor injuries.  In addition to the 3,000 General Power Ultilty homes that lost power due to the tornado, about 3,800 Pennsylvania Power and Light homes lost power throughout the county mainly due to fallen trees.

A severe thunderstorm in Douglass Township tore a 12 foot by 20 foot chunk of the roof from Cynthia's Greshville Inn and deposited it on Pennsylvania State Route 562.  The inn was still structurally sound, but suffered water damage from the ensuing heavy rain that accompanied and postceded the thunderstorm.  A pine tree and several chimneys were damaged around the inn.
Power lines were downed along Rt. 22 in Smithfield Township.

Heavy lake effect snow accumulated an average of two to three feet over a 7 day period.  Total storm amounts ranged from 21 inches in Conneautville (Crawford County) to 55 inches in Edinboro (Erie County).  The storm downed numerous trees and limbs, some on buildings, some on power and telephone lines and some across roads, closing them to traffic.  Houses, garages, carports, mobile homes, barns and commercial buildings suffered collapsed or severely damaged roofs from the heavy snow.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey during the evening of the 7th and near Portland Maine on the morning of the 8th.  For the third time this month at least an inch of rain (heavy at times during the evening) fell across extreme Southeast Pennsylvania.  Some urban and poor drainage flooding occurred,  but no serious injuries or damage were reported.  Storm totals included 1.3 inches at the Franklin Institute (Philadelphia), 1.24 inches in Doylestown and Neshaminy Falls(Bucks County), 1.22 inches in Southampton (Bucks County), 1.14 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.12 inches in Crum Creek (Delaware County).

Structural damage to homes and businesses was reported due to strong winds. Numerous trees were also downed.

Trees were downed by strong winds near Martinsburg.

Heavy rain associated with a low pressure system that developed near the Gulf of Mexico on the 25th and moved through Eastern Pennsylvania the morning of the 26th caused flooding along the Brandywine Creek.  The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 10 a.m. to noon EST on the 26th.  It crested at 7.24 feet at 11 a.m. EST.  A car became stuck in the creek's flood waters in Honey Brook and the flooding forced the closure near the intersection of Pennsylvania State Routes 100 and 926.   Urban flooding also forced the closure of the intersection of Pennsylvania State Routes 100 and 52.  Storm totals included 2.05 inches in Crum Creek (Delaware County), 1.69 inches in Glenmoore, 1.64 inches in Honey Brook and 1.59 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.  No serious injuries were reported.
A severe thunderstorm knocked over trees and power lines in Brecknock Township.
Numerous boats were overturned and damaged at a marina near Raystown Lake.

An intensifying storm system formed off the Virginia coast late at night on the 5th and eventually moved to a position east of Cape Cod by the afternoon of the 6th.  This system produced a band of heavy wet snow from portions of the Poconos in northeast Pennsylvania into New York state.     Snowfall amounts of 8 to 14 inches occurred in the higher elevations of Wayne county during the morning of the 6th.  Preston township and Equinunk received 13 to 14 inches, while Pleasant Mount and Lake Ariel got 8 to 10 inches.     The heavy wet nature of the snow downed many trees and power lines throughout the county.

Light snow fell Thanksgiving Day, one of the busiest travel days of the year. Although only 1 to 2 inches fell across the region, there were hundreds of traffic accidents and many injuries. Interstate 78 in Lebanon County was closed for hours.

6-10 inches of snow fell across Somerset, Cambria, and western Blair Counties closing Rt. 350 between Phillipsburg and the Bald Eagle intersection. 6.8 inches of snow fell on northern Schuylkill and 5.8 inches fell on Harrisburg, breaking the snowfall record for the date.

6-10 inches of snow fell across Somerset, Cambria, and western Blair Counties closing Rt. 350 between Phillipsburg and the Bald Eagle intersection. 6.8 inches of snow fell on northern Schuylkill and 5.8 inches fell on Harrisburg, breaking the snowfall record for the date.
7.1 inches of snow fell on Mt. Davis. Away from the mountain, snowfall decreased rapidly with just 4 inches falling on Confluence and 2 inches on Somerset.

A strong and slow moving cold front brought heavy rains to northeast Pennsylvania from the early evening of the 1st into the early morning of the 2nd.  Generally, from 1 to 3 inches of rain were observed across the region.     Heavy rainfall combined with already saturated soil conditions to produce flash flooding in Bradford county.  Late in the evening of the 1st, the Towanda Creek came out of its banks with several roadways in the town of Canton inundated.  After midnight, the Sugar Run Creek also left its banks with several ensuing bridge and road washouts.  By mid morning on the 2nd, all area creeks and streams had receded back within their banks.       Main stem river flooding followed around daybreak on the 2nd in the Susquehanna basin of northeast Pennsylvania.  Minor lowland flooding occurred from Towanda downstream to Wilkes-Barre.  Other than a few brief evacuations of trailer parks in Wyoming county, only flood plain areas right along the river took on any substantial water.     By the pre-dawn hours of the 5th, all river points had fallen below flood stage.

A weak low pressure system formed along a stationary front during the morning of the 31st across Western Pennsylvania.  As it moved east, it spread some light precipitation across the Lehigh Valley.  While it was warm enough above the ground for the precipitation to fall as rain, air temperatures at the surface were below freezing and freezing rain occurred.  The storm total at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was only three-hundredths (0.03) of an inch.  But, it didn't take much freezing rain on untreated roadways to help cause accidents through the afternoon of the 31st.  Most occurred in Northampton County.One of the worst accidents occurred in Bethlehem when a 33-year-old male lost control, crashed and was trapped within his vehicle.  Another driver in Bethlehem crashed into a concrete barrier.  A pregnant woman was injured in North Plainfeild Township.  Other accidents were reported in Bath, Forks Township (4 injuries as a minivan spun sideways into oncoming traffic.), Hanover and Moore Townships.
The second severe thunderstorm of the day tore down wires in the central part of the county, especially in Whitehall Township.  About 2,000 homes lost power.

An intensifying low pressure system off the New Jersey coast, runoff from the heavy rain and an upstream wind with the rising tide caused minor to locally moderate tidal flooding along the Delaware River during the morning of the 6th.  The tide on the Delaware River at Pier 9 in Philadelphia reached 8.9 feet above mean low water just before 11 a.m. EST, a departure of about 3 feet above the normal astronomical tide.  At these levels some minor flooding affected the lowest points in the Navy Yard, the Gulf Oil area, near the mouth of the Schuylkill River and between the International Airport and Essington (Delaware County).

Some urban and poor drainage flooding occurred,  but no serious injuries or damage were reported.  Storm totals included 1.3 inches at the Franklin Institute (Philadelphia), 1.24 inches in Doylestown and Neshaminy Falls(Bucks County), 1.22 inches in Southampton (Bucks County), 1.14 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.12 inches in Crum Creek (Delaware County).
Trees down along Rt. 75 near Port Royal.
A downburst caused damage to trees and structures.

A severe thunderstorm uprooted several trees close to the Rolling Turf Golf Course in Schwenksville.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down power lines in the northeast part of the county in North Bangor, Pen Argyl and Roseto.  About 1,400 Pennsylvania Power and Light Customers lost power.

A severe thunderstorm blew apart a barn and threw a shed into a parked vehicle within Maxatawny Township.  In addition, about 1,250 homes lost power in the township because of downed trees and power lines.
A severe thunderstorm tore down telephone and electrical wires in Caln Township.
The second severe thunderstorm of the day tore down trees and power lines across the northeast part of the county.
Very strong winds preceded and accompanied a cold front that moved through the area during the evening of the 8th.  Several trees were reported uprooted within Philadelphia.
